---
title: Emit and handle events
description: Declare an event, emit it from a method, and run an in-app handler when it fires.
sidebar:
  order: 3
---

import { Aside, CardGrid, LinkCard } from '@astrojs/starlight/components';

**Task:** announce that something changed so clients (and optionally other
methods of your app) can react.

## Declare an event

An event type is an enum marked `#[app::event]`. Wire it into your state with the
`emits` argument so the macro knows which events the app produces:

```rust
#[app::state(emits = for<'a> Event<'a>)]
pub struct KvStore {
    items: UnorderedMap<String, LwwRegister<String>>,
    handler_counter: Counter,
}

#[app::event]
pub enum Event<'a> {
    Inserted { key: &'a str, value: &'a str },
    Updated { key: &'a str, value: &'a str },
    Removed { key: &'a str },
    Cleared,
}
```

## Emit it

Call `app::emit!` with a variant. Clients subscribed to the context receive it:

```rust
app::emit!(Event::Updated { key: &key, value: &value });
```

## Run a handler when it fires

To also invoke one of your own methods when the event is emitted, use the tuple
form `(event, "handler_name")`. The handler is a regular method named by that
string; its parameters match the event variant's fields. This pattern is
`apps/kv-store-with-handlers`:

```rust
#[app::logic]
impl KvStore {
    pub fn set(&mut self, key: String, value: String) -> app::Result<()> {
        if self.items.contains(&key)? {
            app::emit!((
                Event::Updated { key: &key, value: &value },
                "update_handler"
            ));
        } else {
            app::emit!((
                Event::Inserted { key: &key, value: &value },
                "insert_handler"
            ));
        }
        self.items.insert(key, value.into())?;
        Ok(())
    }

    // handler: name matches the string above; params match the variant fields
    pub fn update_handler(&mut self, key: &str, value: &str) -> app::Result<()> {
        app::log!("Handler 'update_handler' called: key={key}, value={value}");
        self.handler_counter.increment()?;
        Ok(())
    }

    pub fn insert_handler(&mut self, key: &str, value: &str) -> app::Result<()> {
        app::log!("Handler 'insert_handler' called: key={key}, value={value}");
        self.handler_counter.increment()?;
        Ok(())
    }
}
```

<Aside type="note" title="Handlers can mutate state">
  A handler is just another method, so it can write state — here it bumps a
  `Counter`. Use the plain one-argument `emit!` when you only want to notify
  clients, and the tuple form when you also want in-app logic to run.
</Aside>

## Exercise it

```sh
meroctl --node node1 call set \
  --context <context-id> --args '{"key": "hello", "value": "world"}'
```

The matching handler runs as part of the same call, and the emitted event is
published on the node's event stream for subscribed clients.

:::caution
Events are consumed by clients over the node's API rather than by `meroctl`
itself; check your node's API docs for the exact subscription endpoint.
:::

## Worked example: a chat notification flow

A messaging app emits `MessageSent` whenever a member posts, and a web client
subscribes over WebSocket to render new messages the moment they land. The event
and the method that emits it:

```rust
#[app::event]
pub enum Event<'a> {
    MessageSent { from: &'a str, text: &'a str },
}

#[app::logic]
impl Chat {
    pub fn send_message(&mut self, from: String, text: String) -> app::Result<()> {
        self.messages.push(text.clone())?;
        app::emit!(Event::MessageSent { from: &from, text: &text });
        Ok(())
    }
}
```

A client subscribes by sending a single frame on the node's WebSocket endpoint,
naming the contexts it wants events for. The `method` is `subscribe` and the
context ids go under `params.contextIds`:

```json
{ "id": 1, "method": "subscribe", "params": { "contextIds": ["<context-id>"] } }
```

After a `send_message` call commits, every subscriber to that context receives a
state-mutation frame. The application event rides inside it under `data.events`:

```json
{
  "contextId": "<context-id>",
  "type": "StateMutation",
  "data": {
    "newRoot": "<root-hash>",
    "events": [
      { "kind": "MessageSent", "data": [123, 34, 102, 114, 111, 109, 34], "handler": null }
    ]
  }
}
```

Each entry in `events` has three fields:

- `kind` is the variant name — here `"MessageSent"`.
- `data` is the variant's fields serialized to JSON, delivered as a **byte
  array**. Decode the bytes back to UTF-8 and parse them to read the fields.
- `handler` is the in-app handler name when you emitted with the tuple form
  above, or `null` for a plain `emit!`.

```js
const ev = frame.data.events[0];                      // { kind, data, handler }
const { from, text } = JSON.parse(
  new TextDecoder().decode(new Uint8Array(ev.data)),  // -> {"from":"alice","text":"hi"}
);
renderMessage(from, text);                            // update the UI
```

<Aside type="note" title="Bytes, not an inline object">
  The event payload arrives as a JSON array of bytes rather than a nested object
  because the node treats each app's event `data` as opaque — it never parses
  your event schema. The variant name in `kind` tells the client which shape to
  decode the bytes into.
</Aside>
